step_by_step_reasoning: We first identify the user clients: iOS and Android devices, mapping to UserConsumerMobile. The central dashed box represents Snap's EKS infrastructure consisting of Gateway (GW), Media Service, Friend Graph, Snap DB, and Messaging Connection Service (MCS). On the left, we map CloudFront and S3 for media storage/distribution. On the right, ElastiCache acts as the connection store, and at the bottom, DynamoDB serves as the primary metadata database. We divide the architecture into two main workflows: Flow 0 for sending snaps and metadata persistence, and Flow 1 for delivering notifications and checking connection state.

graph.name: Snapchat High-Scale Ephemeral Messaging Architecture
graph.notes: Snap's backend runs across 900+ EKS clusters, serving over 300 million daily active users. Snap DB abstracts DynamoDB to handle TTL, transactions, and ephemeral logic.

nodes.name: EKS API Gateway
nodes.notes: Central ingress routing for active client sessions.

nodes.name: Media Delivery Service
nodes.notes: Handles processing and routing of incoming snap media.

nodes.name: Friend Graph
nodes.notes: Computes user relationships and delivery targets.

nodes.name: Snap DB
nodes.notes: Custom caching/transaction layer to optimize DynamoDB workloads.

nodes.name: Messaging Connection Service
nodes.notes: Manages persistent connections to clients for real-time delivery.

nodes.name: CloudFront CDN
nodes.notes: Caches regional media closer to users for ultra-low latency retrieval.

nodes.name: Amazon S3
nodes.notes: Persistent object storage for ephemeral snap media files.

nodes.name: Amazon DynamoDB
nodes.notes: DATA_PEEK: Stores 400TB+ metadata. Nightly scans read 2B rows/minute to prune expired data.
